pages.title: The functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}_Z(X;F, G)\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{\bullet}_Z(F, G)\)
pages.html: VI The functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}_Z(X;F, G)\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{\bullet}_Z(F, G)\)

blocks.label: VI
blocks.html: VI The functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}_Z(X;F, G)\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{\bullet}_Z(F, G)\)

pages.title: Generalities
pages.html:
1 Generalities

1.1

Let \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(Z\) be a locally closed subset of \(X\). Let \(F\) and \(G\) be \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules; we shall denote by \({\operatorname{Ext}_Z^i}(X;F, G)\) (resp. \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^i}(F, G)\)) the \(i\)-th derived functor of the functor \(G \rightsquigarrow \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\) (resp. \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\)).

Lemma 1.2

The sheaf \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^i}(F, G)\) is canonically isomorphic to the sheaf associated with the presheaf \(U\rightsquigarrow {\operatorname{Ext}_{Z\cap U}^i}(U;F_{|U}, G_{|U})\).

This results from (T, 3.7.2) and from the fact that \(\Gamma(U;\underline{\Gamma}_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)))\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\Gamma_{Z\cap U}(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{{\mathscr{O}_X}_{|U}}(F_{|U}, G_{|U}))\)

Theorem 1.3 (Excision theorem)

Let \(V\) be an open set of \(X\) containing \(Z\); we then have an isomorphism of cohomological functors

\[ \tag{1.3.1} \operatorname{Ext}_{X}^{\bullet}(X;F, G) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}_{V}^{\bullet}(V;F_{|V}, G_{|V}) \]

Indeed, if \(G^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(G\), then \(G^\bullet_{|V}\) is an injective resolution of \(G_{|V}\). The theorem follows immediately.

1.4

Let \(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}\) be the \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module defined by the following conditions (G, 2.9.2): \({\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}}_{|X{-} Z}=0\) and \({\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}}_{|Z}={\mathscr{O}_X}_{|Z}\). We have seen that for every \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(H\), there exists a functorial isomorphism: \(\Gamma_Z(H)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}, H)\). We therefore deduce from it functorial isomorphisms in \(F\) and \(G\):

\begin{align*} \tag{1.4.1} \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)) &\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)),\\ \tag{1.4.2} \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)) &\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} F, G),\\ \tag{1.4.3} \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)) &\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}, G))=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \underline{\Gamma}_Z(G)). \end{align*}

It follows in particular from (1.4.2) that there exists an isomorphism \(\partial\) functorial in \(F\) and \(G\) between \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^i(X;F, G)\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}_{{\mathscr{O}_X}}^i(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} F, G)\)

1.5

By definition the functor \(G \rightsquigarrow \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\) is the composite of the functor \(G \rightsquigarrow\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)\) and the functor \(\Gamma_Z\). Since \(\Gamma_Z\) is left exact (I, 1.9) and since \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)\) is flasque if \(G\) is injective, and since \(\Gamma_Z\) is exact on flasque sheaves (I, 2.12), it follows from (T, 2.4.1) that there exists a spectral functor abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^\bullet(X;F, G)\) and whose initial term is \(H_Z^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q{}_{(F, G))}\).

On the other hand, it follows from (1.4.3) that \(\Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\) is the composite of \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z\) and the functor \(H \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, H)\).

Since the functor \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z\) transforms injectives into injectives (I, 1.4), it follows from (T, 2.4.1) that there exists a spectral functor abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^\bullet(X;F, G)\) and whose initial term is \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(X;F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(G))\).

Finally, it follows from (1.4.2) and from the spectral sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) that there exists a spectral functor abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}_Z(X;F, G)\) and whose initial term is \(H^p(X;\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^\bullet(F, G))\). Whence the

Theorem 1.6

There exist three spectral functors abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^\bullet(X;F, G)\) and whose initial terms are respectively

\begin{align*} \tag{1.6.1} &H_Z^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(F, G))\\ \tag{1.6.2} &H^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^q(F, G))\\ \tag{1.6.3} &\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(X;F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(G)). \end{align*}

1.7

Let now \(Z'\) be a closed subset of \(Z\) and let \(Z''=Z {-} Z'\). We have an exact sequence

\[ \tag{1.7.1} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

which generalizes the exact sequence of (G, 2.9.3). This exact sequence splits locally; we therefore have, for every \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), another exact sequence:

\[ \tag{1.7.2} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0} \]

Let now \(G\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module; if we apply the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\bullet, G)\) to the exact sequence (1.7.2), we deduce from (1.4.2) and from the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) the following theorem:

Theorem 1.8

Let \(Z\) be a locally closed subset of \(X\), \(Z'\) a closed subset of \(Z\) and \(Z''=Z{-} Z'\). We then have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\) and \(G\):

\begin{gather*} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{Z'}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_Z(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{Z''}(F, G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{Z'}}^1(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots{}\\ {}\cdots \operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^i(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{Z''}}^i(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{Z'}}^{i+1}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots{} \end{gather*}
Corollary 1.9

Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\) and let \(U=X{-} Y\). We then have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\) and \(G\):

\begin{gather*} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_Y(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{{\mathscr{O}_X}_{|U}}(F_{|U}, G_{|U}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{Y}^1(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots{}\\ {}\cdots \operatorname{Ext}_{{\mathscr{O}_X}}^i(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{{\mathscr{O}_X}_{|U}}}^i(F_{|U}, G_{|U}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{Y}^{i+1}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \end{gather*}

This corollary is an immediate consequence of theorem (1.3) and of theorem (1.8).

blocks.html: 1 Generalities

Let \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(Z\) be a locally closed subset of \(X\). Let \(F\) and \(G\) be \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules; we shall denote by \({\operatorname{Ext}_Z^i}(X;F, G)\) (resp. \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits_Z^i}(F, G)\)) the \(i\)-th derived functor of the functor \(G ightsquigarrow \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\) (resp. \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\)).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2

The sheaf \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^i}(F, G)\) is canonically isomorphic to the sheaf associated with the presheaf \(U\rightsquigarrow {\operatorname{Ext}_{Z\cap U}^i}(U;F_{|U}, G_{|U})\).

This results from (T, 3.7.2) and from the fact that \(\Gamma(U;\underline{\Gamma}_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)))\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\Gamma_{Z\cap U}(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{{\mathscr{O}_X}_{|U}}(F_{|U}, G_{|U}))\)

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.3 (Excision theorem)

Let \(V\) be an open set of \(X\) containing \(Z\); we then have an isomorphism of cohomological functors

\[ \tag{1.3.1} \operatorname{Ext}_{X}^{\bullet}(X;F, G) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}_{V}^{\bullet}(V;F_{|V}, G_{|V}) \]
Indeed, if \(G^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(G\), then \(G^\bullet_{|V}\) is an injective resolution of \(G_{|V}\). The theorem follows immediately.

Let \(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}\) be the \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module defined by the following conditions (G, 2.9.2): \({\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}}_{|X{-} Z}=0\) and \({\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}}_{|Z}={\mathscr{O}_X}_{|Z}\). We have seen that for every \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(H\), there exists a functorial isomorphism: \(\Gamma_Z(H)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}, H)\). We therefore deduce from it functorial isomorphisms in \(F\) and \(G\):

\begin{align*} \tag{1.4.1} \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)) &\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)),\\ \tag{1.4.2} \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)) &\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} F, G),\\ \tag{1.4.3} \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)) &\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}, G))=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \underline{\Gamma}_Z(G)). \end{align*}

It follows in particular from (1.4.2) that there exists an isomorphism \(\partial\) functorial in \(F\) and \(G\) between \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^i(X;F, G)\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}_{{\mathscr{O}_X}}^i(\mathscr{O}_{X, Z}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} F, G)\)

By definition the functor \(G ightsquigarrow \Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\) is the composite of the functor \(G ightsquigarrow\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)\) and the functor \(\Gamma_Z\). Since \(\Gamma_Z\) is left exact (I, 1.9) and since \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G)\) is flasque if \(G\) is injective, and since \(\Gamma_Z\) is exact on flasque sheaves (I, 2.12), it follows from (T, 2.4.1) that there exists a spectral functor abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^ ullet(X;F, G)\) and whose initial term is \(H_Z^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q{}_{(F, G))}\).

On the other hand, it follows from (1.4.3) that \(\Gamma_Z(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G))\) is the composite of \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z\) and the functor \(H \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, H)\).

Since the functor \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z\) transforms injectives into injectives (I, 1.4), it follows from (T, 2.4.1) that there exists a spectral functor abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^\bullet(X;F, G)\) and whose initial term is \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(X;F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(G))\).

Finally, it follows from (1.4.2) and from the spectral sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) that there exists a spectral functor abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}_Z(X;F, G)\) and whose initial term is \(H^p(X;\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^\bullet(F, G))\). Whence the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.6

There exist three spectral functors abutting to \(\operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^\bullet(X;F, G)\) and whose initial terms are respectively

\begin{align*} \tag{1.6.1} &H_Z^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(F, G))\\ \tag{1.6.2} &H^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^q(F, G))\\ \tag{1.6.3} &\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(X;F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(G)). \end{align*}
Let now \(Z'\) be a closed subset of \(Z\) and let \(Z''=Z {-} Z'\). We have an exact sequence

\[ \tag{1.7.1} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]
which generalizes the exact sequence of (G, 2.9.3). This exact sequence splits locally; we therefore have, for every \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), another exact sequence:

\[ \tag{1.7.2} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z''} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} \mathscr{O}_{X, Z'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0} \]

Let now \(G\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module; if we apply the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\bullet, G)\) to the exact sequence (1.7.2), we deduce from (1.4.2) and from the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.8

Let \(Z\) be a locally closed subset of \(X\), \(Z'\) a closed subset of \(Z\) and \(Z''=Z{-} Z'\). We then have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\) and \(G\):

\begin{gather*} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{Z'}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_Z(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{Z''}(F, G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{Z'}}^1(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots{}\\ {}\cdots \operatorname{Ext}_{Z}^i(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{Z''}}^i(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{Z'}}^{i+1}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots{} \end{gather*}

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.9

Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\) and let \(U=X{-} Y\). We then have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\) and \(G\):

\begin{gather*} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_Y(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{{\mathscr{O}_X}_{|U}}(F_{|U}, G_{|U}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{Y}^1(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots{}\\ {}\cdots \operatorname{Ext}_{{\mathscr{O}_X}}^i(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{{{\mathscr{O}_X}_{|U}}}^i(F_{|U}, G_{|U}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{Y}^{i+1}(F, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \end{gather*}
This corollary is an immediate consequence of theorem (1.3) and of theorem (1.8).

pages.title: Applications to quasi-coherent sheaves on preschemes
pages.html:
2 Applications to quasi-coherent sheaves on preschemes

Proposition 2.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme; for every locally closed part \(Z\) of \(X\), for every coherent Module \(F\) and every quasi-coherent Module \(G\) on \(X\), the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^i(F, G)\) are quasi-coherent.

One shows, as for (1.6.3), that the Modules \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^i(F, G)\) are the abutment of a spectral sequence with initial term \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q (G))\). By (II, corol. 3), the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(G)\) are quasi-coherent, and hence so are the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p (F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q (G))\), since \(F\) is coherent. The proposition then follows immediately.

Now let \(Y\) be a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\) and \(\mathscr{I}\) an ideal of definition of \(Y\). Let \(m\) and \(n\) be integers such that \(m\geqslant n \geqslant 0\); we denote by \(i_{n, m}\) the canonical map \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_m} = \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{m+1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{n+1} = \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\) and by \(j_n\) the map \(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\). The \((\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, i_{n, m})\) form a projective system and the \(j_n\) are compatible with the \(i_{n, m}\).

By applying the functor \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(F\otimes\bullet, G)\), one deduces from this a morphism

\[ \varphi'\colon \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;F\otimes \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;F \otimes \mathscr{O}_{X, Y}, G); \]

it is a morphism of cohomological functors in \(G\). The morphism

\[ \varphi\colon \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;F\otimes \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_Y^i(X;F, G) \]

composed of \(\varphi'\) and \(\theta\) (cf. 1.4) is therefore likewise a functor of cohomological morphisms in \(G\).

One defines in the same way

\[ \underline{\varphi}\colon \varinjlim_n \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(F \otimes \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X;F, G) \]
Theorem 2.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\) defined by a coherent ideal \(\mathscr{I}\), \(F\) a coherent Module, \(G\) a quasi-coherent Module. Then,

  1. \(\underline{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism.

  2. If \(X\) is noetherian, \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.

Since the proof of b) is almost word for word that of (II, 6, b)), thanks to the spectral sequence 1.6.2, we will not reproduce it.

For the proof of a), one may, by (2.1), assume \(X\) affine with ring \(A\), \(F\) (resp. \(G\)) defined by an \(A\)-module \(M\) (resp. \(N\)) and \(\mathscr{I}\) by an ideal \(\underline{\rm{i}}\). It suffices to prove that the homomorphism

\[ \tag{2.3.1} \varinjlim_{{n}} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(M/\underline{\rm{i}}^nM, N) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_Y^i(X, F, G) \]

deduced from \(\underline{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism

Indeed, for \(i=0\), one can canonically identify the two sides of (2.3.1) with the submodule of \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(M, N)\) defined by the elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(M, N)\) annihilated by a power of \(\underline{\rm{i}}\). One then sees that the homomorphism (2.3.1) is none other than the identity map.

The functor \(N\rightsquigarrow \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^\bullet(M/\underline{\rm{i}}^nM, N)\) is a universal \(\partial\)-functor. We shall show that the same holds for the functor \(N \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{Ext}_Y^\bullet(M, N)\). Indeed, if \(N\) is an injective module, by (9 and 11), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^q(N)=0\) if \(q \neq 0\); and by (IV.2.3), \(H_Y^{\circ}(N)\) is injective.

It then follows that \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(X;M, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^q(N))=0\) if \(p+q \neq 0\); one therefore has, by (1.6.3), \(\operatorname{Ext}_Y^i(M, N)=0\) for \(i\neq 0\) and \(N\) injective. This completes the proof.

blocks.html: 2 Applications to quasi-coherent sheaves on preschemes

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme; for every locally closed part \(Z\) of \(X\), for every coherent Module \(F\) and every quasi-coherent Module \(G\) on \(X\), the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Z^i(F, G)\) are quasi-coherent.

One shows, as for (1.6.3), that the Modules \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits_Z^i(F, G)\) are the abutment of a spectral sequence with initial term \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits_Z^q (G))\). By (II, corol. 3), the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits_Z^q(G)\) are quasi-coherent, and hence so are the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p (F, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits_Z^q (G))\), since \(F\) is coherent. The proposition then follows immediately.

Now let \(Y\) be a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\) and \(\mathscr{I}\) an ideal of definition of \(Y\). Let \(m\) and \(n\) be integers such that \(m\geqslant n \geqslant 0\); we denote by \(i_{n, m}\) the canonical map \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_m} = \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{m+1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{n+1} = \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\) and by \(j_n\) the map \(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\). The \((\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, i_{n, m})\) form a projective system and the \(j_n\) are compatible with the \(i_{n, m}\).
By applying the functor \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(F\otimes\bullet, G)\), one deduces from this a morphism

\[ \varphi'\colon \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;F\otimes \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;F \otimes \mathscr{O}_{X, Y}, G); \]
it is a morphism of cohomological functors in \(G\). The morphism

\[ \varphi\colon \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;F\otimes \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_Y^i(X;F, G) \]
composed of \(\varphi'\) and \(\theta\) (cf. 1.4) is therefore likewise a functor of cohomological morphisms in \(G\).
One defines in the same way

\[ \underline{\varphi}\colon \varinjlim_n \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(F \otimes \mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Y^i(X;F, G) \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\) defined by a coherent ideal \(\mathscr{I}\), \(F\) a coherent Module, \(G\) a quasi-coherent Module. Then,

  1. \(\underline{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism.

  2. If \(X\) is noetherian, \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
Since the proof of b) is almost word for word that of (II, 6, b)), thanks to the spectral sequence 1.6.2, we will not reproduce it.

For the proof of a), one may, by (2.1), assume \(X\) affine with ring \(A\), \(F\) (resp. \(G\)) defined by an \(A\)-module \(M\) (resp. \(N\)) and \(\mathscr{I}\) by an ideal \(\underline{\rm{i}}\). It suffices to prove that the homomorphism
\[ \tag{2.3.1} \varinjlim_{{n}} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(M/\underline{\rm{i}}^nM, N) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_Y^i(X, F, G) \]
deduced from \(\underline{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism

Indeed, for \(i=0\), one can canonically identify the two sides of (2.3.1) with the submodule of \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(M, N)\) defined by the elements of \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(M, N)\) annihilated by a power of \(\underline{\rm{i}}\). One then sees that the homomorphism (2.3.1) is none other than the identity map.

The functor \(N\rightsquigarrow \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^\bullet(M/\underline{\rm{i}}^nM, N)\) is a universal \(\partial\)-functor. We shall show that the same holds for the functor \(N \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{Ext}_Y^\bullet(M, N)\). Indeed, if \(N\) is an injective module, by (9 and 11), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^q(N)=0\) if \(q \neq 0\); and by (IV.2.3), \(H_Y^{\circ}(N)\) is injective.

It then follows that \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^p(X;M, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits_Y^q(N))=0\) if \(p+q eq 0\); one therefore has, by (1.6.3), \(\operatorname{Ext}_Y^i(M, N)=0\) for \(i eq 0\) and \(N\) injective. This completes the proof.
